quotes.verbatim_quote:
Two voices in motion while sustaining a third, requires a few long reaches. If at first some of the reaches are too great,
it is permissible to treat the half note as though it were a quarter note—to let go of the half note after sounding the
triad. However, endeavor to sustain the half note full value.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes Van Eps's core multi-voice technique rule: release the sustained voice only as a last resort, because full-value sustain is the load-bearing physical skill the chapter is training.

quotes.topic: Hearing voice motion beats physical execution
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Hearing and understanding the voice motion even though it is slightly disconnected is just as important as being able
to physically sound all of the notes as written, because the harmonic knowledge is necessary.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the method's pedagogical hierarchy where ear/analytical understanding of voice motion is co-equal with the mechanical execution of sustained triads.

quotes.topic: Voice motion mastery as improvisation prerequisite
quotes.verbatim_quote:
The ability to recognize, analyze, and assimilate melodic and harmonic voice motion is very necessary to the
art of improvisation.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames the entire Super and Sub series as not merely a chord-drill but as the analytical foundation for improvisation, the book's ultimate target skill.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In all of these studies, it’s very necessary to note the subtle differences in shapes and notation when
changing from one set to another - some shapes and fingerings will be identical, but the notation is
not.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Warns the student that identical fingerings carry different harmonic/notational meaning across string sets, a load-bearing observation for the 7-string string-set system that drives Van Eps's whole method.

quotes.topic: Transpose to all keys, both minors
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Place in all keys - Major and both Minors - sustain the middle voice - reverse the direction of voice

motion = open to closed
quotes.why_load_bearing: Encodes the standing instruction that every closed-to-open voicing study must be transposed across major and both minor forms and run in both directions, making the variation set non-optional.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
2nd Finger
Arched

Whil

Flat
sustaining "G'

Strings Lo

suxiain the “G". Thix type of Fingering

is found in bars:7-9-11

ulso in many other places
quotes.why_load_bearing: Documents the specific arched-2nd-finger left-hand mechanism Van Eps requires to sustain the middle voice during open-voicing motion, a recurring physical pattern across the chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Repeating steps of any study over and over is very good for finger control
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the practice rule that single steps from any Super/Sub study should be looped as finger-control drills, generalizing the chapter's examples into a practice methodology.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
- Examples of left hand finger team articulation -
The deleted middle voice of the Ist inversion triad that begins each station is marked with a cross = +
In example 1, the entire one octave scale is played on string sets shown in bar 1
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the cross (+) notational convention for omitted middle voice and anchors the finger-team articulation framework used throughout the harmonic minor examples.

quotes.topic: Apply team principles to all scales
quotes.verbatim_quote: Apply these team principles to the melodic Minor - mixed Minor - Major etc
quotes.why_load_bearing: Generalizes the finger-team articulation principle beyond harmonic minor, instructing the student to transfer the technique to every scale form the book treats.
